section.title: The Coming Glory of the House
section.sectionRationale: The second dated oracle, on the twenty-first day of the seventh month, 520 BC, less than a month after the work began. The older builders, remembering Solomon's temple, are discouraged by the humble result. God answers their despair with a threefold Be strong, the promise of His presence, and the astonishing word that the latter glory of this house will surpass the former.
section.thesis: To a people crushed by the smallness of what they are building, God says Be strong, for I am with you, and promises that He will shake the nations and fill this house with a glory greater than the former.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the discouragement over a temple that seems like nothing, met by Be strong and My Spirit remains among you; and the promise that God will shake the nations and fill the house with surpassing glory and peace.

units.label: Be strong, for I am with you
units.note: does it not appear like nothing

verses.text: On the twenty-first day of the seventh month, the word of the LORD came through Haggai the prophet, saying:
“Speak to Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and to Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and also to the remnant of the people. Ask them,
‘Who is left among you who saw this house in its former glory? How does it look to you now? Does it not appear to you like nothing in comparison?’

But now be {{chazaq|strong}}, O Zerubbabel, declares the {{lordOfHosts|LORD}}. Be strong, O Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest. And be strong, all you people of the land, declares the LORD. Work! For I am with you, declares the LORD of Hosts.
This is the promise I made to you when you came out of Egypt. And My {{ruach|Spirit}} remains among you; do not be afraid.”

units.label: I will fill this house with glory
units.note: the latter glory will be greater

verses.text: For this is what the LORD of Hosts says: “Once more, in a little while, I will {{shake|shake}} the heavens and the earth, the sea and the dry land.
I will shake all the nations, and they will come with all their {{chemdat|treasures}}, and I will fill this house with {{kavod|glory}}, says the LORD of Hosts.
The silver is Mine, and the gold is Mine, declares the LORD of Hosts.
The latter glory of this house will be greater than the former, says the LORD of Hosts. And in this place I will provide {{shalom|peace}}, declares the LORD of Hosts.”

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, to take courage, to hold firm. It is the word God spoke to Joshua before entering Canaan and David spoke to Solomon before building the first temple. Here it rings out three times, one for each hearer, turning discouraged builders into a courageous, working people.

lordOfHosts.gloss: The LORD of Hosts, or LORD of Armies, commander of the ranks of heaven and all the powers of creation. The title is stacked again and again through this short oracle, grounding every command and promise in the limitless power of the God who speaks.

ruach.gloss: Breath, wind, spirit. My Spirit remains among you recalls the presence of God that led Israel from Egypt and filled the tabernacle. The same Spirit who empowered the first exodus abides with this small remnant, so they need not fear.

shake.gloss: To quake, tremble, shake violently. God will shake heaven, earth, sea, and nations, an image of His sovereign upheaval of all things. Hebrews 12:26-28 takes up this word to point to the final shaking that leaves only the unshakable kingdom.

chemdat.gloss: That which is desired, precious, or treasured. The phrase can be read as the treasures of the nations flowing in, or, in an older reading, the desire of all nations, understood by many as pointing to the Messiah. Either way the nations' true wealth is brought to honor God's house.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, honor, splendor. The kavod once filled Solomon's temple as a visible cloud. God promises to fill this humble house with a glory that will surpass the former, a promise fulfilled supremely when the Lord Himself entered the second temple in the person of Christ.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being, flourishing. More than the absence of conflict, it is the full harmony God gives. In this place I will provide peace points beyond the building to the peace with God secured in Christ, who is our peace.

basic.context: Less than a month after the rebuilding began, the work has slowed. The oldest people can remember Solomon's magnificent temple, destroyed decades earlier, and next to that memory the new one looks like nothing. God speaks into that discouragement, telling the builders three times to be strong, reminding them He is with them, and promising a glory greater than anything they have seen.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real word to real builders on a dated day, and it carries a promise that unfolds in stages across history. The point for us is not that our church buildings will be grand, but that God delights to fill small and humble beginnings with His own glory, and that His people should take courage because He is present and at work far beyond what they can see.
basic.originalLanguages: God says be strong, chazaq, the same word spoken to Joshua and to Solomon before great tasks, and He says it three times. He promises His Spirit, ruach, remains among them. He will shake, ra'ash, the nations, and fill the house with glory, kavod, the weighty splendor that once filled Solomon's temple. And He promises peace, shalom, the wholeness that finally comes in Christ.
basic.typology: The former glory was the cloud of God's presence in Solomon's temple. The promised greater glory points to Jesus, who entered this second temple in person, God's glory in the flesh. The shaking of the nations and the gathering of their treasures foreshadow the nations streaming to God through the gospel.
basic.christ: God promised that the latter glory of this house would be greater than the former. That was fulfilled not in gold and silver but when Jesus, the glory of God in human form, walked into the second temple and taught there. He is greater than the temple. Through Him the nations are gathered, and in this place, in Christ, God provides the deepest peace, peace with God Himself.
basic.god: God sees the discouraged and speaks courage to them. He does not despise the day of small things; He promises to fill humble beginnings with His glory. He is sovereign over the nations, able to shake heaven and earth, and He is generous, for all the silver and gold are already His. Above all, He is present with His people by His Spirit.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 12 quotes the shaking of heaven and earth and points to the kingdom that cannot be shaken. Ezra 3 records the older men weeping at the modest foundation. And Zechariah 4 asks who dares despise the day of small things.
basic.oneStory: This promise carries the temple story forward: a humble house that will one day hold a glory greater than Solomon's, because the Lord Himself will come to it. It looks ahead to Christ, the true glory, and to the nations gathered and given peace, and finally to the new creation, the house filled with the glory of God forever.

context.literary: The second of four dated oracles, and the first of the two encouraging, forward looking messages. It answers the discouragement voiced in verse 3 with a rising sequence: a threefold be strong, the reassurance of God's presence and Spirit, and then the sweeping cosmic promise of verses 6-9, punctuated repeatedly by the title LORD of Hosts, which lends divine weight to every clause.
context.historical: The word came on the twenty-first day of the seventh month, during the Feast of Tabernacles, when Israel remembered the wilderness and the first temple's dedication under Solomon had once occurred. Some present were old enough to have seen Solomon's temple before its destruction in 586 BC, and the contrast with the modest rebuild, noted also in Ezra 3:12-13, sapped the people's resolve.
context.canonical: The promise of shaking the nations is directly cited in Hebrews 12:26-28 and read there as pointing to the unshakable kingdom. The greater glory and the peace given in this place are drawn into the New Testament's presentation of Christ as the true temple and our peace, and into the vision of the nations gathered to worship God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lift a discouraged, comparison weary people out of despair by anchoring them in God's presence, His covenant faithfulness since the exodus, and His sovereign promise to fill their humble work with a glory and peace far surpassing the former temple.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific promise to the builders of the second temple; it does not prescribe material splendor for churches. The abiding truths are that God is present with His discouraged people, that He does not despise small beginnings, and that His purposes reach a glory we cannot yet see.

debates.question: What is the desired of all nations, or the treasures of the nations, in verse 7?

views.label: The treasures or wealth of the nations
views.case: The grammar and the parallel with silver and gold in verse 8 favor the nations' precious things being brought to honor God's house, as the BSB renders it.

views.label: The Messiah, the Desire of nations
views.case: An older and cherished reading takes the singular sense as a person, the One whom the nations long for, fulfilled when Christ, the true treasure and glory, came to the temple.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on Christ: whether the nations' wealth flows in or the Desire of nations Himself comes, the house is filled with a glory that finds its center in the Lord who enters it.

debates.question: How is the latter glory greater than the former?

views.label: Fulfilled ultimately in Christ's presence
views.case: The second temple never rivaled Solomon's in splendor, so the greater glory is best understood as the coming of the Lord Himself, God's glory in the flesh, into that very temple.

views.label: Including the temple's later expansion and the gathering of nations
views.case: Herod's lavish enlargement and the streaming of Gentiles toward the God of Israel also express a widening glory, all of it climaxing in Christ.

debates.bearing: The promise was not about outshining Solomon in gold but about the surpassing glory of God present among His people, realized when Jesus taught in that house and secured peace for the nations.

study.originalLanguages: The oracle is built on courage and glory. Three times God says chazaq, be strong, take hold, the very word He gave Joshua before Canaan and David gave Solomon before building the temple. He assures them His ruach, His Spirit, remains among them as at the exodus, so they need not fear. He will ra'ash, shake, the heavens, earth, sea, and nations, a sovereign upheaval that Hebrews 12 takes up. The chemdah of the nations, their desired treasures, will come in, and He will fill the house with kavod, the weighty glory that once filled Solomon's temple. And in this place He will give shalom, the wholeness and peace that finds its fullness in Christ.

typology.type: The glory that filled the former temple
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the glory of God in the flesh, who entered the second temple in person, John 1:14 and Malachi 3:1.

typology.type: The shaking of the nations and their treasures brought in
typology.fulfillment: The nations gathered to God through the gospel and offering their worship, Revelation 21:24-26.
Christ Himself, who is our peace and makes peace by His cross, Ephesians 2:14-17.

study.christ: The promise that the latter glory would surpass the former was fulfilled not in gold but in a Person. The Lord whom they sought came suddenly to His temple, Malachi 3:1, when Jesus, the very glory of God, was carried into it as a child, taught within its courts, and cleansed it as His Father's house. He is greater than the temple, Matthew 12:6, the true meeting place of God and humanity. Through Him the nations are gathered, their treasures laid before God, and in this place, in Christ, God provides the deepest peace, reconciliation with Himself, for He is our peace, Ephesians 2:14.
study.god: God is tender toward the discouraged, meeting their despair with a threefold command to be strong and the assurance of His presence. He does not despise small beginnings but promises to fill them with His glory. He is utterly sovereign, able to shake heaven, earth, and nations, and He owns all the wealth of the world, so His purposes never lack resources. And He is the giver of peace, providing in His house the shalom that no human effort can secure.

crossReferences.note: The older men who had seen the first temple wept at the foundation of the second, the very discouragement this oracle addresses.
Quotes God's promise to shake heaven and earth and points to the kingdom that cannot be shaken.
The Lord will suddenly come to His temple, pointing to the greater glory fulfilled in Christ's coming.
Christ is our peace and builds Jew and Gentile into a holy temple, God's dwelling by the Spirit.

study.oneStory: This promise moves the temple story toward its climax. A house that looked like nothing would hold a glory greater than Solomon's, because the Lord Himself would enter it. It points to Christ, the true glory and our peace, to the nations gathered in, and finally to the new creation, where the whole city is filled with the glory of God and there is no temple, for the Lord God and the Lamb are its temple.

sermon.title: Take Courage: You Build More Than You See
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly on Haggai 2:1-9, surveying the whole book to set the context and then dwelling on God's threefold call to take courage, His promise of presence, and the promise to fill the house with glory greater than the former, tracing its fulfillment in Christ and the new creation. A rich, orthodox exposition of this passage.

quiz.q: Why were the builders so discouraged in this passage?

options.text: The new temple seemed like nothing compared to the remembered glory of Solomon's temple.
options.note: Verse 3 names the comparison with the former house as the root of their despair.

options.text: They had run completely out of building materials.
options.note: The text points to discouragement from comparison, not a shortage of supplies.

options.text: Their enemies had attacked the work again.
options.note: Earlier opposition stalled the work, but here the issue is the smallness of the result.

options.text: God had told them to stop building.
options.note: The opposite: God tells them to be strong and keep working.

quiz.q: What is the heart of God's encouragement in verses 4-5?

options.text: Be strong and work, for I am with you and My Spirit remains among you.
options.note: The threefold be strong rests on God's presence and abiding Spirit.

options.text: Try harder and the building will impress everyone.
options.note: The comfort is God's presence, not the promise of an impressive result by effort.

options.text: Do not worry, the work does not really matter.
options.note: The work does matter; God calls them to it and promises to be with them in it.

options.text: Wait until conditions improve before working.
options.note: God says Work! now, precisely because He is with them.

quiz.q: How is the promise that the latter glory will surpass the former best understood?

options.text: Fulfilled supremely when the Lord Himself, God's glory in the flesh, came to that temple in Christ.
options.note: The second temple never outshone Solomon's in gold; the greater glory was Christ's presence, Malachi 3:1.

options.text: The second temple would eventually hold more gold than Solomon's.
options.note: There is a widening glory, but the deepest fulfillment is the Lord's own coming, not sheer wealth.

options.text: It refers only to a future millennial temple.
options.note: Some look ahead, but the New Testament centers the fulfillment on Christ present in that very house.

options.text: The promise was never fulfilled.
options.note: Scripture presents it as fulfilled in Christ, the glory who entered the temple.

quiz.q: What does God's shaking of the heavens, earth, and nations reveal?

options.text: His sovereign power to overturn all things and gather the nations to Himself.
options.note: The shaking shows God's rule over creation and the nations, taken up in Hebrews 12.

options.text: That the world will simply fall into random chaos.
options.note: It is God's purposeful, sovereign act, not random chaos.

options.text: That God is angry and merely wants to destroy.
options.note: The shaking serves His saving purpose, filling His house with glory and giving peace.

options.text: That earthly kingdoms are the ones truly in control.
options.note: The point is the opposite: God shakes the nations; He is in control.

quiz.q: What does in this place I will provide peace ultimately point to?

options.text: The peace with God secured in Christ, who is Himself our peace.
options.note: The shalom of God's house finds its fullness in Christ, Ephesians 2:14.

options.text: A period of political calm for the nation of Judah.
options.note: There may be a near horizon, but the promise reaches to the peace given in Christ.

options.text: Simply the absence of noise during worship.
options.note: Shalom means wholeness and reconciliation, far more than quiet.

options.text: A peace that people must earn by their building.
options.note: God provides the peace; it is His gift, fulfilled in Christ.

questions: The builders were paralyzed by comparing their work to a greater past. Where does comparison steal your courage to keep serving God faithfully?
God said be strong three times before He said anything about glory. How does hearing God's I am with you change the way you face a task that feels too small or too hard?
God does not despise the day of small things. What small, unimpressive act of obedience might God be asking you to keep at, trusting Him for the outcome?
The greater glory came when the Lord Himself entered the temple. How does knowing that Christ is the true glory reshape what you hope for in your church and your service?
God promises peace in His house, fulfilled in Christ our peace. Where do you most need to receive that peace with God rather than trying to manufacture it yourself?

translationNotes.note: This is the promise I made to you when you came out of Egypt ties God's present assurance to His covenant word at the exodus; some render it as a reference to that covenant, My word stands.

They will come with all their treasures renders a phrase also famously translated the Desire of all nations, taken by many older readers as a title for the Messiah; the sense of glory brought to God's house holds in either reading.
The latter glory of this house will be greater than the former; glory renders kavod, the weighty splendor of God's presence, fulfilled when the Lord Himself came to the temple.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Haggai 2:1-9. All 9 verses reproduced in order. BSB footnote at 2:6 notes the citation in Hebrews 12:26.
